aliases: Grilled Mackerel and Chicken Skewer Bento
Japanese Mackerel and Chicken Bento Box

english: Grilled Mackerel and Chicken Skewer Bento
Japanese Mackerel and Chicken Bento Box

food_subgroup: Mixed protein bento — grilled fish and grilled chicken with rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bento food product class. No specific saba-yakitori combination bento exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific saba-yakitori bento code; A0CKP (composite meal / bento) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Japanese bento combination.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box portion of approximately 450 g, comprising grilled mackerel fillet, two to three yakitori chicken skewers, steamed white rice, and a small portion of mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Atlantic mackerel (raw, NDB 15046), Chicken thigh meat grilled (NDB 05165), White rice cooked (NDB 20045), and mixed vegetables; weighted by estimated component proportions (mackerel 20%, chicken 20%, rice 50%, vegetables 10%).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for saba-yakitori chicken bento in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA branded foods. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component blending: grilled Atlantic mackerel (~20% by weight), grilled chicken thigh (~20%), cooked white rice (~50%), mixed vegetables (~10%). Mackerel values adjusted for grilling (approximately 15% moisture loss). Sodium elevated to reflect soy-sauce-based tare marinade typical of yakitori preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed Japanese bento meals — analogous estimate based on cooked white rice GI (Jasmine/short-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database; protein and fat co-ingestion effect applied per Brand-Miller et al. (2003).
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for saba-yakitori chicken bento. White rice (short-grain, cooked) has a GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database). Co-ingestion of protein (~11.8 g/100 g) and fat (~4.2 g/100 g) from mackerel and chicken is well-documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response. Applying a conservative mixed-meal attenuation factor consistent with Brand-Miller et al. (2003, Am J Clin Nutr), the composite meal GI is estimated at 54, which falls into the 'low' GI category (GI <= 55). GL per 450 g serving = (54 × 76.05 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = approximately 41.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from soy-sauce-based yakitori tare marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g bento box provides an estimated 1395 mg sodium, representing approximately 60% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium tare or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by white rice content, partially attenuated by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated meal GI of 54 and GL of approximately 41.1 per bento box indicate a low glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, consider substituting brown rice if available, and pair the meal with non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from grilled mackerel offset by moderate sodium from tare
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled Atlantic mackerel is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective; however, the elevated sodium content from yakitori tare warrants attention, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should balance the omega-3 benefit against sodium load by choosing lower-sodium preparation options where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saba and Yakitori Chicken Bento (サバと焼き鳥弁当) is a Japanese-style bento box meal combining two distinct protein preparations: grilled Atlantic mackerel (saba, 鲭鱼) and yakitori chicken skewers (焼き鳥), served alongside steamed white rice and a small portion of mixed vegetables. Saba is a fatty, oily fish prized in Japanese cuisine for its rich umami flavour and high omega-3 content; it is typically seasoned with salt or miso and grilled until the skin is lightly charred and crisp. Yakitori refers to bite-sized pieces of chicken — commonly thigh meat — threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal or a gas flame, basted repeatedly with a sweet-savoury tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The combination of grilled fish and grilled chicken in a single bento box is popular in Japanese fast-casual and convenience dining, offering a high-protein, moderate-fat meal with a satisfying balance of smoky, savoury, and slightly sweet flavours. Nutritionally, the bento provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 11.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate. The mackerel component contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with cardiovascular and cognitive health benefits. The chicken thigh contributes niacin, vitamin B6, and additional protein. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the soy-sauce-based tare marinade used in yakitori preparation. The estimated glycemic index of the meal is 54 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content relative to a plain rice meal. This bento is suitable for most adults as part of a balanced diet, with sodium monitoring recommended for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and portion control advised for those managing Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Japanese bento box?
Is mackerel bento good for heart health?
What is the glycemic index of a Japanese bento?
Is saba fish high in omega-3?
How much sodium is in yakitori chicken?
What is yakitori sauce made of?
Is saba the same as mackerel?

confidence_reason: No exact match for saba-yakitori chicken bento found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA branded foods. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component blending of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic mackerel, grilled chicken thigh, cooked white rice, and mixed vegetables. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with mixed-meal attenuation applied. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved-source entry for this exact bento combination was identified. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or a future HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Sodium is estimated at 310 mg/100 g, reflecting typical yakitori tare marinade contribution; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for grilled mackerel and grilled chicken thigh; naturally zero in these minimally processed animal protein components. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate, categorized as 'low'; no direct GI measurement for this bento combination was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually confirmed by a single source entry. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; yakitori tare typically contains mirin and sake (alcohol), rendering this dish non-halal by default unless specifically prepared with halal-certified ingredients. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002473 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for this specific bento combination exists in publicly available ontology releases as of April 2026.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Constructed canonical English food name, added Japanese local name, built nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central, estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database, populated health context and LLM fields.

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index 54 (<= 55); recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving; updated health_context type2_diabetes concern_level and guidance accordingly.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 aliases consistency failure — audited every string in aliases_by_language against flat aliases array; ensured '鯖魚烤雞串便當' (traditional Chinese with traditional final character 當) appears verbatim in flat aliases; confirmed all 15 flat alias entries match aliases_by_language entries exactly character-for-character; removed duplicate entry and standardised the flat aliases list to 15 unique entries all present in aliases_by_language.
